1: viYricho sogeret umusegeret: ‘Jericho was shut up and barred’ — the doubled construction (sogeret participle + musegeret passive) is emphatic: utterly closed, impenetrably locked. The city that no human force could open is the very city YHWH declares already given (natatih) in v2 — a divine perfect treating the future as accomplished fact. The gap between the city’s locked state and YHWH’s already-given declaration frames the entire chapter as a lesson in what faith receives that sight cannot yet see.
2: reeh natatih beyadecha: ‘See — I have given it into your hand’ — the Hebrew perfect (natatih) is past tense: YHWH speaks of Jericho’s fall as already accomplished. This is the divine perspective from outside time applied to events that Israel must still walk out through seven days of marching. The same tension appears throughout OT and NT: God speaks of the elect as “foreknown,” of justification as already complete, of the resurrection as certain — while the historical unfolding remains ahead. Joshua 6:2 is the OT basis for speaking of future realities in the past tense of divine certainty.
3: usbavtem et hair kol anshei hamilchamah: ‘march around the city, all the men of war’ — military language (anshei hamilchamah, men of war) surrounds an operation involving no weapons: walking in circles while priests blow rams’ horns. The deliberate incongruity between military personnel and non-military action signals the point: this is YHWH’s battle, not Israel’s. The armies are present as participants in a divine act, not as agents of the victory. The same principle governs apostolic ministry: “we do not wage war according to the flesh” (2 Corinthians 10:3), but the weapons are not less powerful for being spiritual.
4: shiva kohanim yisu shiva shofarot hayovelim: ‘seven priests shall carry seven rams’ horns’ — the shofar hayovel (Jubilee horn) is distinct from the chatsotsrot (straight silver trumpets of Num 10). The yovel root connects to Jubilee (yovel, Lev 25) — the year of liberty and land-return. Whether intentional or not, the instrument announcing Jericho’s fall is the instrument of liberation; YHWH’s conquest is framed as a Jubilee-act, a reclaiming of the land from its temporary occupants. The sevenfold repetition (seven priests, seven horns, seven days, seven circuits) saturates the narrative with sabbatical completion.
5: bimshoch beqeren hayovel bishmaachem et qol hashofar yaria chol haam: ‘when they draw out the blast on the ram’s horn, the whole people shall shout with a great shout’ — the sustained blast (bimshoch, drawing out the sound) precedes the corporate shout (yaria). The mechanics require the people to wait in silence through six days and then coordinate a single simultaneous shout. The obedient synchrony of the people’s shout with the divine signal is the human act corresponding to divine action: the shout does not cause the walls to fall, but the walls fall at the shout.
6: Joshua’s direct communication with the priests and army implements YHWH’s command precisely. The detailed delegation — priests carry the ark, priests blow the horns, armed men march before and behind — creates a liturgical procession: ark-centered, priest-mediated, encompassed by armed men carrying no weapons against the city. This is the structure of biblical worship translated into military terms: the holy object at the center, priestly mediation, and the people surrounding it in ordered attention.
7: ivru usubvu et hair: ‘advance and march around the city’ — the Hebrew avar (cross over, advance) is the same verb used for Israel’s crossing of the Jordan and for Israel’s entry into the land generally. Every march around Jericho is a small avar — an act of crossing, passage, committed movement. The narrative is full of crossings: Jordan, Jericho, the land. Each individual crossing participates in the larger crossing-dynamic of the entire conquest.
8: The priests with their rams’ horns march before the ark, blowing continuously (tamid taqea bataqoa hashofar). The continual sounding is both a sonic claim on the territory — declaring YHWH’s sovereignty by the Jubilee-instrument — and a liturgical frame within which the silent army marches. The silence of the army against the continual priestly music creates an unusual soundscape: worshipful proclamation surrounding military formation. This is not primarily a siege but a liturgy.
9: hame'aseph holech acharei haaron: ‘the rear guard came after the ark’ — the ark travels in the middle of the procession, with armed men ahead and a rear guard behind. This is the opposite of a normal military formation where the ark would be guarded at the rear; here the ark leads, and the military presence surrounds it as an escort. The spatial arrangement communicates: YHWH leads the battle; human forces are his retinue.
10: lo taria velo tashmiu et qolchem: ‘you shall not shout or make any sound with your voice’ — six days of absolute silence, broken only by priestly horns. The commanded silence is a discipline of waiting: Israel cannot shout until YHWH says to shout. The prohibition on premature noise is a prohibition on premature action — the very temptation Israel succumbed to at Massah and Meribah and at the golden calf. Silence before YHWH is a form of trust that his word and timing are sufficient.
11: Joshua executed the command precisely: a single circuit, return to camp, silence. The single-circuit days build expectation without providing discharge. Six days of partial action — enough to establish the pattern but not enough to produce the result. The daily manna, the weekly Sabbath, the annual Passover — all are repetitions building toward a final completion. Jericho’s six days build toward the seventh day’s seven circuits.
12: vayashkem Yehoshua baboker: early rising — the same phrase used of Abraham rising early to obey YHWH’s command regarding Isaac (Genesis 22:3). Early rising as indicator of readiness and obedience is a minor but consistent motif: the one who has truly received a divine command does not delay its execution. The priests and ark are ready; the process begins again with the same fidelity as day one.
13: The repetition of seven priests with seven horns marching and blowing continually establishes the narrative as a liturgical cycle. The reader must inhabit the repetition, not skip past it. Six times the narrative rehearses the march, horn-blowing, silence, and return; the sixth repetition makes the seventh day’s deviation — seven circuits instead of one — structurally explosive. The storyteller uses liturgical repetition to generate narrative momentum.
14: One circuit per day, silence, return to camp — the formula exactly repeated. The six-day structure recalls the six days of creation followed by the seventh day of completion. Jericho’s fall on the seventh day after six days of preparation is structurally a Sabbath-pattern: what the preparation builds toward arrives on the day of rest and completion.
15: bayom hashvii vayashkimu kaolot hashachar: ‘on the seventh day they rose at the dawning of the day’ — the earliest possible start, the longest possible day. Seven circuits instead of one, beginning at dawn. The seventh day breaks the pattern established by days one through six in every dimension: earlier start, seven circuits, the shout that was forbidden now commanded. The Sabbath is the day when the pattern that seemed locked is broken open.
16: bitoqa hashofar yaria hadam: ‘at the trumpet blast, shout!’ — Joshua’s command at the seventh blast of the seventh circuit connects action (shout) to divine signal (horn). The timing matters: not premature, not delayed, but synchronized with the divine signal. The shout is Israel’s act of faith that YHWH’s word has been fulfilled — they shout before the walls fall, trusting that the walls will fall. This is the structure of Hebrews 11:30: “by faith the walls of Jericho fell down after they were encircled for seven days.”
17: tihyeh hair cherem hi vekol asher bah laYHWH: ‘the city shall be a cherem — devoted to YHWH’ — cherem is the chapter’s central theological term. It means something set apart entirely for YHWH, irrevocably consecrated, not for human use. The same root is used of things offered to the sanctuary that cannot be redeemed (Leviticus 27:28-29). For Jericho, cherem means total destruction: no survivors, no plunder, everything given to YHWH — destroyed (living things) or placed in the sanctuary treasury (precious metals). Jericho as the firstfruits city receives firstfruits treatment: everything holy, nothing retained.
18: rak shemertu mihaacherem: ‘but keep away from the devoted things’ — the warning about cherem contamination: taking any cherem object makes the entire camp cherem. The contamination is moral, not physical — the sin of treating what belongs to YHWH as available for personal use. The same principle governs the Lord’s Supper: “whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty of profaning the body and blood of the Lord” (1 Corinthians 11:27) — treating the holy as ordinary brings judgment.
19: kol kesef vezahav uklei nechoshet uvarzel qodesh hem laYHWH: ‘all silver and gold and vessels of bronze and iron are sacred (qodesh) to YHWH’ — the precious metals are not destroyed but consecrated; they pass into YHWH’s treasury as firstfruits of the conquest. Consistent with the principle that the first of everything belongs to YHWH (Exodus 13:2): Jericho as the first city has its wealth wholly consecrated. Later cities allow plunder; this one does not.
20: vayyari ham vayitqeu bashofarot vatipol hachomah tachhteha: ‘the people shouted and the wall fell flat’ — grammatically, the people’s shout and the wall’s fall are juxtaposed but the wall’s fall is in the passive: it fell, was caused to fall. The human action (shout) and the divine action (wall falls) are presented together without causal explanation. The gap between human act and divine result is preserved deliberately: this was YHWH’s act in response to Israel’s faith-act.
21: vayacharimu et kol asher bair: ‘they devoted to destruction all that was in the city’ — the execution of cherem against Jericho’s population is among the most difficult passages in the OT. Several observations: (1) The Canaanite nations had been warned through four centuries of continued iniquity (Genesis 15:16); (2) the narrative shows the way of escape was open — Rahab’s household is saved; (3) the cherem is explicitly not ethnic but covenantal — the command targets religious contamination, not ethnicity (Deuteronomy 20:17-18). The theological issues require sustained engagement with divine character and purpose, not quick harmonization.
22: The spies are given their assignment: rescue Rahab as promised. The keeping of the oath given to a prostitute in a city subjected to cherem is itself remarkable: YHWH’s people honor their word even in the chaos of total destruction. The specificity of the rescue (bring out the woman and all who belong to her, just as you swore) demonstrates covenant faithfulness in action: promises made are promises kept, regardless of circumstances.
23: vayotziu et Rachav ve et aviha ve et imah ve et acheiha: ‘they brought out Rahab, her father, mother, brothers, and all who belonged to her’ — the household salvation is complete. The promise in Joshua 2:18-19 was precisely this: all who are in your house will be saved. The inclusion of the entire household under her faith-act illustrates the household-salvation principle that reappears in Acts: “believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household” (Acts 16:31).
24: veha'ir sarefu ba'esh: ‘they burned the city with fire’ — the burning after the cherem is the physical completion of the consecration: what cannot be taken cannot remain. Archaeological evidence at Tell es-Sultan (likely ancient Jericho) includes extensive burning evidence at levels corresponding to the Late Bronze Age, though the exact stratigraphy remains contested. The silver, gold, and metals go to YHWH’s treasury; everything else becomes ash.
25: ve et Rachav hazonah ve et beit aviha ve et kol asher lah hecheyah Yehoshua: ‘Joshua spared Rahab the prostitute, her father’s household, and all who belonged to her’ — the explicit mention of her profession (ha-zonah) in the rescue verse is pointed: what determined her salvation was not social respectability but faith-expressing-itself-in-risk. Hebrews credits her by faith (Hebrews 11:31); James by works (James 2:25) — both are correct because her faith was expressed through her works. Matthew places her in the Messiah’s genealogy (Matthew 1:5).
26: arur ha'ish lifnei YHWH asher yaqum uvana et hair hazot et Yericho: ‘cursed before YHWH is the man who rises up and rebuilds this city Jericho’ — the rebuilding curse extends the cherem: because Jericho was devoted to YHWH, it must not be reclaimed for ordinary use. The specific curse formula (at the cost of his firstborn he lays its foundation, at the cost of his youngest son he sets up its gates) was fulfilled four centuries later: “In his days Hiel of Bethel built Jericho. He laid its foundation at the cost of Abiram his firstborn, and set up its gates at the cost of his youngest son Segub, according to the word of the LORD that he spoke by Joshua” (1 Kings 16:34).
27: vayhi YHWH et Yehoshua vayhi shime'o bechol haAretz: ‘the LORD was with Joshua, and his fame spread throughout the land’ — the divine presence formula used of Moses and of Israel generally. The spread of Joshua’s fame is the reverse of Rahab’s report (Joshua 2:9-11): the nations heard and their hearts melted; after Jericho, that melting intensifies. The same power that kept YHWH’s promise now makes YHWH’s purpose known even to those who resist it.
